author: @philipvecchio3292
text: I was reading into rectennas, rectifier antennas, which take radio waves and convert them into usable electricity. Usable electricity. A lot of the circuits are very similar to crystal radio circuits, and they're used a lot of times for RFID chips, but there's been increased. Talk about using them for different types of low power devices. I'm sure those smoke detectors in particular could use that type of power. I could also imagine lighting, in particular ambient lighting using a rectenna, a transistor, a photoresister, capacitor that would charge up all day using ambient radio waves and then light at night.

author: @russellzauner
text:
I'm not a semiconductor representative but the LTC3588-1 (Nanopower Energy Harvesting Power Supply) has been around since before Analog Devices purchased Linear Technology.
Several online maker/DIY sites have already populated breakout boards for sale and your favorite site likely does as well.
I took one (I got mine at Sparkfun, I'm not an affiliate) and a couple blank PCB copper clad panels, soldered a wire to each of them, connected it to the chip, bang I was getting 100mA easy off the fluorescent lights in the lab at work (and that was in 2012).

author: @davide8982
text:
Hi,
Imagine that you have a Sterling engine that you get to temperature mechanically until a resistance heats up the air container? As soon as the gets air reaches a high enough and sustainable temperature, the air will continuously contract and expand. If the energy generated by the Sterling engine could self-sustain the air chamber temperature, the movement will be ad eternum?
I’m no expert, just a curious mind, maybe there are other ways to achieve the same goal, that’s why I wanna challenge you. Can you figure out a way of using the movement of the Sterling engine to keep it’s air hot enough to sustain uninterrupted movement - a machine that feed on itself?
Cheers

author: @jamest.5001
text:
I guess even putting what is basically a umbrella, covered in aluminum,or copper foil, on a 10'-20' pole, in the top of a couple dozen trees, (the large umbrellas like 6'-9' across) and put about the same number of ground rods, use about 20 gauge wire, to handle way more amps than it will see,  the whole lot connected to a 2-4kv cap about 1 farad each, so the difference in voltage doesn't pull down a higher one, the match the voltage, parallel caps close in voltage. Then use a large super cap bank, 10-20 farad 7-14v,  build a high voltage buck converter. It will possibly take days or a month to charge the cap bank, I can't recall the voltage for atmospheric electricity, 1kv per 100 meters or something, if you had nothing but foil, wire and bamboo, a few tools, some older electronics to scavenge from, you can atleast start a fire ,  as for a more permanent power supply a couple dozen little wind turbines  using 250-500 watt brushless motors would be better than nothing, use hover board wheel motors , to build vawts,  about 60"-72" diameter, abd 24" thick, made from steam flattened bamboo, dried and treated with teak oil!  A bit like a waterwheel on its side, only much lighter,  using strips of bamboo to make square dowel rods, to build the structure from steam  bent in layers to be glued together making scoops about the same way. 
If they average 18vdc and 4-5 amps per hour, that is 100 watts +/- a few watts,  times 24, that's 2400 watts! A poor village in Africa or south America could live on that, use the power for a 6-9 watt light bulb in each hut, and a refrigerator for the village to keep food fresh in. There is  not many that poor anymore, slightly scaling it up, and a few pedal generators,  they could have more, maybe put a water wheel in the river. Spin a 2-3 kw generator, every house get a 100-300 ah battery, let the system charge the batteries, then runs ac cord to the house from the inverter, with a breaker 10-15a (5-10a for230-240v)  a community kitchen would be better, with locked sections in a  large fridge, and separate freezer, this is running astray from power scavenging,  the first time I connected solar panels to a battery bank attached to a inverter, making my own power! It was an amazing feeling! I love it✌️

author: @azena.
text:
Hang on, can I check my understanding here? The variable capacitor that you demonstrated there is pumping electrons out of your "earth" and then putting it through the full wave rectifier diode structure and storing those electrons in the other capacitor? Have I got that right?

Why does the variable capacitor have that effect? I assume it's the kinetic energy being converted into stored electrical potential somehow. I can only assume that when the plate is close then electrons are attracted to the bottom plate. The only place these electrons can come from are the earth. But then when the plate is moved away then the electrons are left to dissipate and so... actually, I think it might be the other way around? I feel like something needs to push the electrons through the rectifier circuit, and my gut instinct is that it's the motion of pushing the top plate down towards the bottom plate that does that. Electrons wouldn't naturally dissipate through the rectifier to any great number. Ok, off to google I go.

author: @Jarvis30
text:
Like almost everything Rob shows us with wind & water this past couple years, its not practical for Actually using.  None of them are going to cook a bag of popcorn in your microwave.   I ubdersyand they are concepts but there is no way to scale these concepts up for the average person.
I wish Rob would get back to the chemistry of Battery & capacitor concepts and stop with the childrens science demos.
We are a developed audience and lately we are just being taken backwards.

replies.author: @JamesZike
replies.text:
Everything is useless until someone finds a use for it. Can't say I've seen a practical use for an electron pump realized as of late, but there is potential. Seems to be Rob's ethos, to explore the possibilities, and test the bounds of what he can achieve in his home lab, while learning about the cutting edge of those particular fields (the people finding a use for it). This never felt like a how-to channel, but more like an educational channel, where "childrens science demos" actually sort of fit since he spends more words explaining the principles behind the thing, than the thing he's building. It's kind of there in the name of the channel, isn't it? "Thinking 'n' Tinkering"

As to the applications of wind and water power generation, don't have a clue what you're on about. Wind and water are the two most proven power sources humanity has relied upon since pre-History. And they're not exactly out of date either, given the number of homesteaders and rural household that use them precisely because they're off grid.

I guess it all comes down to application. If you're only interested in cooking a bag of popcorn in a microwave (which can and is done using wind and water power--electrical charges don't care how they were generated, they'll run a microwave just as well as charge a capacitor), and you're in a city or close enough where it's more economical to get wired up, then any investigation into renewable power is something that may not be of interest to you. You just don't have the right application in mind for the potentials Rob seems to be looking into.

Personally, I find Rob's dives into exploring both the general concepts and how they can be applied in built-up areas / why the industrial solutions don't work so well in home settings to be quite informative. I find these videos to also have a fairly low bar to entry into the topics he wants to talk about. idk, personally I think the tradeoff between immediate applicability to broader educational appeal to be a good thing. Most people won't have the resources to get Engineering degrees. I'm always grateful when those with advanced knowledge share it so freely.
